gates.title: API Validation Errors — Resource-Scoped Keys Required
gates.description: API validation error responses must use a resource-scoped key: { "errors": { "<resource>": ["msg"] } }. Never errors.body[]. Enforce via a formatError(resource: string, messages: string[]) utility function so the pattern is impossible to violate accidentally. Returning errors.body[] is a spec deficiency that causes all contract tests against real-world test suites to fail, even when the implementation is otherwise correct.
gates.check:
1. Search codebase for any validation error response that uses a literal "body" key:
   grep -rn '"body"' src/ | grep -i error
2. Confirm a formatError(resource, messages) utility exists and is used at all validation points. 3. Run the project's contract test suite (e.g. Hurl, Postman) — all error-shape tests must pass.
gates.passCriterion: Zero validation error responses use errors.body[]. All use errors.<resource>[]. A formatError() utility enforces the pattern. Contract tests for error shapes pass.
gates.evidence: Hurl RC2 against gothinkster/realworld — 6 of 13 official community contract tests failed against GS Treatment v6. All six failures traced to a single root cause: the v6 spec (conduit-gs.md §3.4) explicitly specified errors.body[] but the Hurl tests expected resource-scoped keys (errors.username[], errors.article[]). Both v6 and the RX control implementation failed identically because the spec was wrong, not the implementations. The GS rubric had scored v6 highly despite this contract violation because the rubric was written by the same author as the spec — neither could see the gap. An independent external test suite found it immediately.

provenance.finding: Hurl RC2 — 6/13 official Conduit contract tests failed due to errors.body[] vs errors.<resource>[]
provenance.discovered_in: GS Treatment v6 — spec gap, not implementation error

gates.title: Nullable Fields — Empty String Must Coerce to Null at Service Layer
gates.description: Optional profile fields declared nullable in the schema must coerce empty-string client input to null at the service layer: bio === '' ? null : bio. An empty string stored as "" violates the nullable contract and breaks contract tests. This coercion must happen in the service, not the route handler, so the domain model always reflects the declared type.
gates.check: 1. Identify all fields in the schema declared as nullable (string | null). 2. Locate the service-layer update function for each such field. 3. Confirm each has a coercion guard: fieldValue === '' ? null : fieldValue 4. Run contract tests covering PUT/PATCH with empty string for nullable fields.
gates.passCriterion: All nullable fields coerce '' to null at the service layer. PUT/PATCH with bio: "" (or equivalent) returns null, not "". Contract tests for nullable field behavior pass.
gates.evidence: Hurl RC1 against gothinkster/realworld — PUT /api/user with bio: "" returned "" instead of null. One test failure. The fix is a single coercion guard at the service layer. Without this gate, the pattern is easy to miss because tsc passes, unit tests pass (they don't send ""), and only a contract test that explicitly sends an empty string catches it. The Conduit spec says bio is nullable; storing "" violates that contract silently.

provenance.finding: Hurl RC1 — PUT /api/user bio:'' returned '' not null; one contract test failure

gates.title: Hurl Contract Tests — Official RealWorld Suite Passes
gates.description: Run the gothinkster/realworld official Hurl contract test suite against a running server. These tests were authored by the open-source community with no knowledge of GS or the implementation. They are the definitive external validator for RealWorld Conduit API implementations. All tests must pass before a cycle closes.
gates.check: 1. Start the server: npm run dev (or equivalent) 2. Clone or use local copy of gothinkster/realworld Hurl specs 3. Run: hurl --test specs/api/hurl/*.hurl --variable host=localhost --variable port=3000 4. All tests must exit 0
gates.passCriterion: hurl --test exits 0. All official RealWorld contract tests pass. Pass rate: 13/13 (or equivalent for non-Conduit APIs: 100% of contract tests pass).
gates.evidence: GS Treatment v6 passed 6/13 official Hurl contract tests. The two root causes were api-error-envelope-resource-scoped (6 failures) and api-nullable-field-coercion (1 failure). Both were spec gaps invisible to the GS rubric. v7 hypothesis: formalizing these as gates and including them in the spec moves the pass rate from 6/13 to 13/13.

provenance.finding: v6: 6/13 Hurl tests pass; v7 hypothesis is 13/13 by closing RC1+RC2

gates.title: Node ESM — CJS Packages Require Default Import Pattern
gates.description: CommonJS packages (jsonwebtoken, bcrypt, multer, and others) do not expose named exports under ESM interop. Named imports compile cleanly under tsc but crash at runtime with "does not provide an export named 'X'". Required pattern: import pkg from 'jsonwebtoken'; const { sign } = pkg. This gate applies to any Node project with "type": "module" or moduleResolution: "NodeNext".
gates.check:
1. Identify all dependencies that are CommonJS-only (no ESM entry in package.json exports map).
   Key candidates: jsonwebtoken, bcrypt, multer, express (v4), require-from-string.
2. Search for named imports of these packages:
   grep -rn "^import {" src/ | grep -E "jsonwebtoken|bcrypt|multer"
3. Any named import of a CJS-only package must be replaced with default import + destructure. 4. Start the server and confirm no runtime "does not provide an export named" errors occur.
gates.passCriterion: Zero named imports of CJS-only packages in ESM projects. Server starts without named-export runtime errors. tsc --noEmit exits 0 AND server starts successfully on first request.
gates.evidence: GS Treatment v6 — server started (tsc --noEmit passed, npm run build succeeded), then crashed on the first JWT operation with "jsonwebtoken does not provide an export named 'sign'". The build gate passed; the runtime gate did not exist. This is a category of failure that tsc cannot catch because TypeScript's ESM interop adds type stubs for CJS named exports — the types exist, the runtime exports do not. The only detection is a runtime smoke test or an explicit import pattern gate. The gate was added after v6; v7 uses default import + destructure throughout.

provenance.finding: v6 server started then crashed on first JWT call — tsc passed, runtime did not

gates.title: Semantic Duplicate Code Detection at End of Cycle
gates.description: At the end of each development cycle, run CodeSeeker's duplicate detection scan to identify semantically similar code blocks (similarity > 0.85). Consolidate duplicates into shared utilities before marking the cycle complete. Prevents the progressive accumulation of near-identical logic that diverges over time, making the codebase harder to maintain and test.
gates.check:
At end of each development cycle (before close_cycle completes):
1. Run CodeSeeker duplicate detection scan on the codebase
2. Review results: any pair with semantic similarity > 0.85 is a candidate for deduplication
3. For each duplicate pair:
   - Confirm they solve the same problem (not just syntactically similar)
   - Consolidate into a shared utility
   - Update all call sites
4. Commit the deduplication before marking cycle complete
gates.passCriterion: No semantically duplicate code blocks with similarity > 0.85 remain in the codebase.
gates.failureMessage: Semantically duplicate code detected. Consolidate before closing the cycle.
gates.fixHint: Use codeseeker_duplicates to identify pairs. Extract shared logic to a utility. Update call sites.

tools.name: codeseeker
tools.purpose: Finds semantically similar code blocks using vector embeddings and AST analysis

gates.title: Orphaned Code Detection at End of Cycle
gates.description: At the end of each development cycle, run CodeSeeker's orphan detection to find modules and functions with no incoming edges in the dependency graph. Remove confirmed dead exports rather than commenting them out. Keeps the codebase lean, reduces cognitive load on reviewers, and prevents stale code from accumulating across cycles.
gates.check:
At end of each development cycle:
1. Run CodeSeeker orphan detection (isolated subgraph scan)
2. Review results: modules/functions with no incoming edges in the dependency graph
3. For each orphaned code unit:
   - Is it a dead export? Remove it.
   - Is it test-only? Move to test files or mark clearly.
   - Is it intentionally standalone? Document why in a comment.
4. Delete orphaned code. Do not comment it out. Git has history.
gates.passCriterion: No isolated subgraphs in the dependency graph. All exported symbols have at least one call site.
gates.failureMessage: Orphaned code detected -- modules or functions with no callers found.
gates.fixHint: Use codeseeker_orphans to identify isolated code. Delete unused exports. Move test helpers to test files.

tools.name: codeseeker
tools.purpose: Finds isolated subgraphs (orphaned modules) in the dependency graph

gates.title: Cyclomatic Complexity — Maximum 10 Per Function
gates.description: No function may have a cyclomatic complexity greater than 10. Complexity above 10 indicates a function that is doing too much, is hard to test completely, and is difficult for an AI to reason about correctly. This is a direct enforcement of the Bounded property: each function has a limited, understandable decision space.
gates.check:
Run ESLint with complexity rule:
  eslint --rule '{"complexity": ["error", 10]}' src/
Or configure in .eslintrc:
  "complexity": ["error", 10]
Fix violations by decomposing the function into smaller, single-purpose functions.
gates.passCriterion: Zero functions with cyclomatic complexity > 10. eslint with complexity rule exits 0.
gates.evidence: High cyclomatic complexity is consistently correlated with defect density (McCabe 1976, replicated across multiple industrial studies). Functions with complexity > 10 require exponentially more test cases for full branch coverage. In AI-assisted development, high-complexity functions are particularly risky: the AI must track more conditional paths simultaneously and is more likely to miss edge cases. The threshold of 10 is the standard industry threshold (SonarQube default, ESLint default).

gates.title: No Duplicate String Literals — Extract to Named Constants
gates.description: String values appearing more than 3 times in the codebase must be extracted to named constants. Duplicate string literals are a proxy for the Bounded property: the same concept being expressed in multiple places without a single authoritative definition. When a value changes, all occurrences must be found and updated manually, which produces drift. A named constant makes the concept explicit and the value single-source-of-truth.
gates.check:
Run SonarJS duplicate string literal detection:
  npx eslint --plugin sonarjs --rule '{"sonarjs/no-duplicate-string": "error"}' src/
Or configure in .eslintrc:
  "sonarjs/no-duplicate-string": ["error", { "threshold": 3 }]
Any string appearing more than 3 times that is not a technical constant (import path, log prefix) must be extracted to a named constant in a constants file.
gates.passCriterion: Zero sonarjs/no-duplicate-string violations at threshold 3. eslint exits 0.
gates.evidence: SonarJS ran against GS experiment conditions with no knowledge of GS or the rubric. Naive condition (no GS guidance): 19 duplicate string literal violations. v3 treatment (GS-guided): 1 violation. The external tool ranked conditions consistently with the GS rubric ranking — without any GS knowledge. This confirmed the rubric is measuring something real: the same signal is detectable by an independent quality model. The gate formalizes what SonarJS was already detecting.

provenance.finding: SonarJS: naive=19 violations, v3 treatment=1 — external tool consistent with GS rubric ranking
provenance.discovered_in: GS Experiment Ax v6 external validation run

gates.title: SonarQube Quality Gate — Independent Static Analysis Passes
gates.description: Run SonarQube (or SonarCloud) static analysis and confirm the Quality Gate passes. SonarQube is an industry-standard quality tool with no knowledge of GS. A passing Quality Gate (A ratings across reliability, security, maintainability; 0 bugs, 0 vulnerabilities) from an independent tool closes Layer 3 of the GS circularity problem: GS-designed implementations scoring well on a non-GS quality model.
gates.check:
Option A — SonarCloud (recommended for open source):
  1. Connect repo to SonarCloud
  2. Run: npx sonar-scanner (with sonar-project.properties configured)
  3. Confirm Quality Gate status = OK in SonarCloud dashboard
Option B — SonarQube local:
  1. docker run -d -p 9000:9000 sonarqube:community
  2. sonar-scanner -Dsonar.host.url=http://localhost:9000
  3. Confirm Quality Gate = OK
Minimum pass criteria: 0 bugs, 0 vulnerabilities, A maintainability rating.
gates.passCriterion: SonarQube/SonarCloud Quality Gate: OK. Ratings: Reliability A, Security A, Maintainability A. Zero bugs, zero vulnerabilities flagged.
gates.evidence: GS Treatment v6 rated A/A/A, 0 bugs, 0 vulnerabilities, Quality Gate OK on SonarQube. This was run as part of the Norman Owens circularity challenge: if a system passes GS-designed tests on a GS-designed implementation scoring against a GS rubric, nothing independent has been tested. SonarQube — with no GS knowledge — agreed the output was high quality. Closes Layer 3 of the circularity problem partially.

provenance.finding: SonarQube: Treatment v6 rated A/A/A, 0 bugs, 0 vulnerabilities, QG OK
provenance.discovered_in: Norman Owens peer review response — external validation run

gates.title: Use Semantic Search Before Grep
gates.description: When searching the codebase for existing utilities, patterns, or similar implementations, prefer CodeSeeker's semantic (vector + graph) search over literal grep. Grep misses conceptually identical code with different naming, leading to duplicate utilities and inflated dependency footprints. This gate enforces the habit of semantic-first search in sessions where CodeSeeker is available.
gates.check:
When searching the codebase for existing code, utilities, patterns, or similar implementations:
1. If CodeSeeker MCP tool is available in the session: use codeseeker_search FIRST
2. Only fall back to grep/glob for exact literal string matches that semantic search won't help with
3. Before writing any new utility function: search semantically ("find utility that does X")
4. Before adding a new dependency: search semantically ("existing code that handles X")
This prevents duplicate code proliferation in large codebases.
gates.passCriterion: CodeSeeker used for conceptual searches. grep reserved for exact string matches.
gates.failureMessage: grep used for conceptual search when CodeSeeker is available, risking missed existing utilities.
gates.fixHint: Add CodeSeeker MCP to .claude/settings.json. Use codeseeker_search for "find code that does X" queries.

tools.name: codeseeker
tools.purpose: Graph-powered semantic code search with duplicate and orphan detection

gates.title: Reuse Dev Docker Containers -- No Duplicate Servers
gates.description: Before spinning up a new Docker container for a development database or service, check whether a container of that type already exists. If one does, create a new database or namespace inside it rather than running a second container. Prevents resource waste, port collisions, and environment drift caused by multiple parallel instances of the same service type.
gates.check:
Before spinning up a new Docker container for development databases or services:
1. Run: docker ps -a --format "{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Image}}"
2. Check if a container for this service type (postgres, redis, mongo, etc.) already exists
3. If exists and running: create a new database/namespace WITHIN the existing container
   - PostgreSQL: CREATE DATABASE {project}_dev within existing postgres container
   - Redis: use a different DB index (SELECT 1, SELECT 2, etc.) per project
   - MongoDB: create a new database within existing mongod
4. If exists but stopped: docker start {container_name}
5. Only create a new container if no existing container of this type exists
6. All dev containers should connect to a shared 'forgecraft-dev' Docker network
7. Document the shared container name and project-specific DB/namespace in docs/dev-environment.md
gates.passCriterion: No duplicate database/service containers running for development. Projects share containers, separate by database/namespace.
gates.failureMessage: New Docker container created when existing container of same type already running.
gates.fixHint: Use `docker ps -a` to find existing containers. Create a new database within the existing container instead of a new container.

gates.title: Check Before Install -- All Package Managers
gates.description: Before installing any package with any package manager, verify it is not already installed or declared. Prevents version conflicts, duplicate entries in manifests, and unnecessary network calls. Applies to npm, pip, brew, choco, apt, go get, and any other package manager in use on the project.
gates.check:
Before installing any package with any package manager (npm, pip, brew, choco, apt, go get):
1. Check if the package is already installed/declared:
   - npm local: check package.json dependencies
   - npm global: npm list -g {pkg}
   - pip: pip list | grep {pkg} OR check pyproject.toml/requirements.txt
   - brew: brew list --versions {pkg}
   - choco: choco list --local-only {pkg}
   - apt: dpkg -l {pkg}
   - go: check go.mod for module
2. Semver check:
   - Same major, older minor/patch: update (upgrade command) or skip
   - Same major, same or newer: skip entirely
   - Different major: only proceed if project explicitly requires this major version (documented)
3. Before adding any new runtime dependency: search semantically for existing packages/utilities
   that already cover the use case (use codeseeker if available, or grep).
gates.passCriterion: No package is installed without first checking if it already exists. No duplicate major versions unless explicitly justified.
gates.failureMessage: Package installed without checking existing installations, or duplicate versions detected.
gates.fixHint: Always check before installing. For npm: check package.json. For others: run the list command for that package manager first.

gates.title: VS Code Extension Version Hygiene
gates.description: Before installing any VS Code extension, check the already-installed list to prevent duplicate major versions and unnecessary re-installs. Keeps the development environment deterministic and avoids conflicting extension behaviour caused by co-existing major versions of the same tool.
gates.check:
Before installing any VS Code extension:
1. Run: code --list-extensions --show-versions
2. Parse output as {id}@{semver} map
3. For each extension to install:
   a. Same ID, same major version exists → update (code --install-extension {id}) or skip if already latest minor
   b. Same ID, different major version → only install if .vscode/extensions.json documents the version requirement with a rationale comment
   c. Multiple copies at same major → deduplicate: keep highest minor, uninstall older ones
4. Never run code --install-extension without first checking installed list
gates.passCriterion: No duplicate extension installs exist at the same major version. All installs were checked against existing list first.
gates.failureMessage: Duplicate VS Code extensions detected or extensions installed without checking existing list.
gates.fixHint: Run `code --list-extensions --show-versions`, remove duplicates with `code --uninstall-extension {id}@{old-version}`, keep highest minor per major.

tools.name: vscode-cli
tools.purpose: List and manage installed VS Code extensions

gates.title: AAVE Health Factor Floor — Liquidation Risk and Borrowing Rate Invariants
gates.description: During simulation runs, assert that the AAVE health factor never drops below 1.05 and that a non-zero borrowing rate is recorded whenever AAVE debt exists. Enforced at both the tick level during the run and in the post-run summary. Prevents invalid parameter sets from being accepted after a silent liquidation approach or a zero-cost borrow anomaly.
gates.check: FIN-07: At every simulation tick where AAVE debt > 0, assert health_factor >= 1.05. A breach means the position approached or crossed the liquidation threshold. The assertion must halt the run immediately on first breach and log: timestamp, health_factor observed, collateral value, debt value, and the tick index. This is a tick-level guard, not a post-run aggregate — the run must not continue past a health_factor < 1.05 event. SIM-04: In post-run output, assert that borrowing_rate > 0 for every hour where AAVE debt > 0. A zero borrowing rate when debt exists indicates that borrowing costs were not modeled, making the PnL calculation invalid. Report total hours with debt and hours with zero borrowing_rate on failure. Both checks are mandatory and independent.
gates.passCriterion: health_factor >= 1.05 at every tick where AAVE debt > 0; run halted immediately on breach. borrowing_rate > 0 for all hours where AAVE debt > 0 in post-run output.
gates.evidence: Strategy ran 200 hours before liquidation. The health factor dropped to 1.03 over a 4-hour window — a gradual degradation that no aggregate post-run metric caught because the run appeared to complete normally (it liquidated at hour 200, not hour 204). A tick-level assertion at health_factor < 1.05 would have halted the run at hour 196, flagged the parameter set as unsafe, and prevented it from being accepted as a valid optimization result. The borrowing rate check would have separately caught a 48-hour window where debt existed but zero borrowing cost was recorded, inflating the PnL.

gates.title: Bootstrap Bypass Detection — Full Position Required for RUNNING State Resume
gates.description: Verify that the state machine's position detection logic requires ALL THREE components (LP position + AAVE debt + short hedge) before resuming the RUNNING state. Also verify that crash reinstatement leaves capital in the wallet undeployed, not pre-deployed as collateral. Prevents recovery paths that short-circuit to RUNNING with an incomplete position, causing the engine to run indefinitely with no productive activity.
gates.check: SM-03: The pre-commit hook verifies that _detect_positions() (or equivalent position detection function) returns False — and does NOT transition to RUNNING — unless ALL THREE conditions are true simultaneously: (1) LP position exists and is in range, (2) AAVE debt > 0, (3) short hedge position exists with size > 0. Partial position detection (e.g., collateral > 0 without LP) must not trigger RUNNING state. The hook enforces that a unit test exists with the following assertion: assert _detect_positions() returns False when LP is missing even if collateral > 0. SM-07: Verify that crash reinstatement logic (the path taken when recovering from an unclean shutdown) leaves capital in the wallet in an undeployed state. Any reinstatement function that pre-deploys collateral or opens positions as part of recovery must be flagged — recovery must restore wallet balance, not replicate a deployed state.
gates.passCriterion: _detect_positions() returns False when any of LP, debt, or short is missing, regardless of other component states. Unit test for LP-missing-but-collateral-present case exists and passes. Crash reinstatement functions do not deploy capital or open positions.
gates.evidence: Recovery path pre-seeded AAVE collateral as part of crash reinstatement. _detect_positions() saw collateral > 0 and short-circuited to RUNNING state with no LP deployed and no hedge. The engine ran for 1 full year emitting zero trades and zero fee income. The bug passed all unit tests because the test fixtures used the same short-circuit: they set collateral > 0 to simulate RUNNING state, masking the fact that LP absence should have prevented that transition. Required test: assert _detect_positions() returns False when LP is missing even if collateral exists.

gates.title: LP Range Entry Guard — Valid Range at Position Creation and Fee/Width Correlation
gates.description: At LP position creation time, assert that the current price is within the specified range (lower < current_price < upper). Also verify that fee income correlates positively with LP range width — a wider range that produces lower fees indicates a broken fee model. Prevents the entire class of "out-of-range from day 1" bugs and fee model inversion anomalies.
gates.check: FIN-06: At every LP position creation event (add_liquidity, mint, or equivalent), assert: lower_tick_price < current_price < upper_tick_price. This is a runtime guard executed at position creation time, not post-run. If violated, raise an exception and log: lower_bound, upper_bound, current_price, and the tick at which the position was created. The pre-commit hook verifies that any function that creates an LP position contains this assertion or calls a validator that includes it. FIN-10: In simulation output, verify that fee_income correlates positively with LP range width (upper - lower). If a parameter sweep shows that wider ranges consistently produce lower fee income than narrower ranges at the same price level, the fee model has an inversion bug. Flag any result set where the Pearson correlation between range_width and fee_income is < 0 across >= 5 parameter sweep entries.
gates.passCriterion: lower_tick_price < current_price < upper_tick_price asserted at every LP position creation. Exception raised and run halted if violated. No negative correlation between LP range width and fee income across parameter sweeps.
gates.evidence: LP deployed out of range immediately earned $0 fees for 800 hours. The entry guard is a 2-line check at position creation time — the entire class of "out-of-range from day 1" bugs is prevented by a single assertion that takes under a millisecond to evaluate. The fee/width correlation check would have additionally caught a fee model bug where the fee calculation used a fixed range width constant, causing wider ranges to report proportionally less fee income despite identical in-range time.

gates.title: Monetary Precision — No Float for On-Chain Financial Math
gates.description: Detect and block the use of float literals and float() casts in code that handles on-chain monetary values. All financial calculations involving wei, token amounts, balances, or fee income must use Decimal or integer arithmetic. Float precision loss accumulates silently over long simulation runs and live deployments.
gates.check:
FIN-01: Pre-commit hook scans files that handle monetary values (identified by any of: imports of web3, eth_account, uniswap, aave, or presence of variable patterns ending in _wei, _amount, _balance, _fee, _income, _cost) for the following patterns: (1) float() calls where the argument is a variable with a monetary suffix
    — pattern: `float\(.*(_wei|_amount|_balance|_fee|_income|_cost)\)`.
(2) Float literals used in arithmetic with monetary variables
    — pattern: `(_wei|_amount|_balance)\s*[\*\/\+\-]\s*[0-9]+\.[0-9]+`.
(3) Direct float arithmetic on variables named with monetary suffixes
    — pattern: `[a-z_]*(wei|amount|balance|fee|income|cost)\s*=\s*[^D][^e][^c].*[\*\/\+\-]`.
Report file, line number, variable name, and the offending expression on failure. Configurable: additional monetary variable suffix patterns in forgecraft.yaml.
gates.passCriterion: Zero float() casts or float literals in arithmetic expressions involving variables with monetary suffixes in files that import web3, eth_account, uniswap, or aave. All monetary math uses Decimal, int (wei), or an audited fixed-point library.
gates.evidence: Float precision loss in an LP fee calculation caused a cumulative accounting error of $340 over 1,000 simulation hours. The error was undetectable without explicit decimal precision tracking — each individual tick calculation was off by less than $0.001, well within plausibility checks, but the error compounded consistently across 1,000,000 ticks. The root cause was a single float() cast converting a Decimal fee amount before summing it into the running total.

gates.title: Optimizer Journal Versioning — Commit SHA Tagging and Audit Metadata
gates.description: Optimizer journals must be tagged with the engine commit SHA at creation time. Journals produced on a different engine version must not be resumed or merged into the same optimization run. All audit database entries must store commit SHA, config hash, and price data version. Prevents optimization results from accumulating on a buggy engine and being accepted as valid.
gates.check: SIG-07 / DP-03: Before resuming or extending any optimizer journal (CMA-ES population state, Bayesian prior, or equivalent), verify that the journal's recorded engine_commit_sha matches the current HEAD commit SHA. If they differ, the journal must not be resumed — it must be archived and a new journal started. The pre-release hook scans all journal files in the optimizer output directory and fails if any journal lacks an engine_commit_sha field or if any journal's SHA differs from HEAD. DP-01: Verify that price history data files are versioned and that the version is recorded in each optimizer journal. Journals referencing different price versions are incompatible and must not be merged. DP-02: Verify that the audit database (optimization results store) contains the following fields for every entry: engine_commit_sha, config_hash, price_data_version, run_timestamp. Entries missing any of these fields must be flagged as unauditable and excluded from parameter selection.
gates.passCriterion: All optimizer journals have engine_commit_sha matching current HEAD. All journals have a price_data_version field. All audit database entries contain engine_commit_sha, config_hash, price_data_version, and run_timestamp. Journals from non-matching SHA are archived, not resumed.
gates.evidence: 130 CMA-ES optimizer runs accumulated on an engine with a known fee calculation bug. The "optimal" parameters discovered by the optimizer were exploiting the bug — they maximized fee income in a model where fees were overcounted by 3x. After the bug was fixed, all 130 journals were invalid but indistinguishable from valid ones without manual inspection. SHA tagging would have automatically invalidated all pre-fix journals on the next run, forcing a fresh optimization on the corrected engine.

gates.title: P&L Decomposition Invariant — Silent Loss Bug Detection
gates.description: After every simulation run, verify that the sum of P&L components equals the total reported P&L. Catches accounting bugs where a component is computed but never wired into the total.
gates.check: After every simulation run, verify: fee_income + price_income + borrowing_cost == total_pnl +/- 1%. This is a Category A silent loss bug check. If the decomposition fails, accounting components are unlinked and the strategy is earning zero while appearing to run normally. Extend to all components present in the strategy's accounting model. The check must name the exact fields it is comparing and report the delta on failure. Also flag runs where total_pnl > 0 but fee_income <= 0 (SIM-08: silent positive return bug — positive total PnL without any fee income indicates unaccounted income source or accounting bypass). borrowing_cost is a required decomposition component alongside fee_income and price_income (FIN-05): it must be present in every run output that involves AAVE debt, and its sign must be negative (it is a cost).
gates.passCriterion: |fee_income + price_income + borrowing_cost - total_pnl| / |total_pnl| < 0.01. Fails immediately with named components and delta if violated. borrowing_cost must be present and non-zero when debt > 0. Runs where total_pnl > 0 and fee_income <= 0 must be flagged as suspect and reviewed.
gates.evidence: Real DeFi simulation case: strategy ran for 1,000+ hours reporting zero PnL. Root cause was that fee_income was computed but never added to total_pnl. The strategy appeared healthy (no errors, no crashes) while silently earning nothing. P&L decomposition check would have caught this in the first test run.

gates.title: Private Key Log Prevention — No Private Keys in Logs or Exception Tracebacks
gates.description: Detect and block code that passes private key variables, mnemonics, or wallet secrets to logging functions, string formatting, or exception messages. Also verifies that .env files containing secrets are included in .gitignore. Prevents credential leakage through log aggregators, error tracking systems, or accidental commits.
gates.check:
OP-01: Pre-commit hook scans all files that import web3 or eth_account for patterns where secret-named variables are passed to output functions: (1) Logging calls with secret variable names
    — pattern: `(log\.|logger\.|logging\.)\w+\(.*\b(private_key|pk|secret|mnemonic|seed_phrase)\b`.
(2) String formatting of secret variables
    — pattern: `(str\(|f"|f'|\.format\().*\b(private_key|pk|secret|mnemonic)\b`.
(3) Exception construction with secret variables
    — pattern: `(raise|Exception|Error)\(.*\b(private_key|pk|secret|mnemonic)\b`.
(4) Print statements with secret variables
    — pattern: `print\(.*\b(private_key|pk|secret|mnemonic)\b`.
Additionally, verify that every .env file in the repository root and subdirectories is listed in .gitignore. A missing .gitignore entry for any .env file fails the hook. Report file, line number, and the matched pattern on failure.
gates.passCriterion: Zero occurrences of secret-named variables passed to log, print, str(), f-string, or exception constructors in files importing web3 or eth_account. All .env files present in the repository are covered by .gitignore entries.
gates.evidence: An exception handler logged the full account object — including the private key field — to stdout using a generic str(account) call inside an except block. In a containerized deployment, stdout is captured by the log aggregator. The private key was present in Datadog logs for 3 weeks before it was discovered during an unrelated audit. The account had to be rotated and all associated positions transferred. A pre-commit scan for str(.*private and logger patterns would have caught the line before it was ever committed.

gates.title: Simulation Engine Health — Error State, Capital Floor, and Activity Check
gates.description: After every simulation run, verify that the engine spent the majority of time in productive states, maintained minimum capital throughout, and produced enough state transitions to confirm genuine activity. Catches engines that silently stall, loop in recovery, or lose all capital while reporting a completed run.
gates.check: After every simulation run, assert all three conditions: (1) SIM-02: fraction of ticks spent in ERROR or RECOVERY state must be < 10% of total simulation ticks. Report exact percentage and tick counts on failure. (2) SIM-03: engine capital must never drop below 10% of starting capital at any recorded snapshot. Report the minimum capital observed and the timestamp. (3) SIM-07: for a full-year simulation run (>= 8,000 hours), total state transitions logged by the engine must exceed 100. A run with fewer transitions indicates the state machine was frozen or bypassed. Report exact transition count on failure. All three checks are mandatory — a run that passes two and fails one is a failed run.
gates.passCriterion: Error/recovery state fraction < 10% of total ticks. Minimum capital >= 10% of starting capital at all snapshots. State transition count > 100 for full-year runs. All three conditions must pass; partial pass is a failure.
gates.evidence: Engine that spent 60% of simulation time in RECOVERY state emitted zero trades while appearing to complete successfully. No exception was raised; the run output was structurally valid. The capital floor check would have caught total-loss scenarios that silently passed as completed runs. The transition count check would have flagged the frozen state machine that never left RECOVERY on the first post-run validation.

gates.title: Simulation LP Fee Nonzero — Fee Income and In-Range Percentage Check
gates.description: After every simulation run with LP (liquidity provision) enabled, verify that fee_income is greater than zero and that the LP in-range percentage is reported and positive. Catches strategies that deploy LP out of range and silently earn nothing for the entire run duration.
gates.check: After every simulation run where LP is enabled in the strategy config, assert: (1) SIM-01: fee_income > $0 for the full run. A completed run with LP enabled and zero fee income is a Category A bug indicator. Report total run hours and fee_income on failure. (2) SIM-06: LP in-range percentage must be present in run output and > 0%. A missing or zero in-range percentage means the LP position was never within the active price range and no fees were earnable at any point. Report in-range% and the LP range bounds (lower, upper) versus the price range observed during the run. Both checks must pass. A run with fee_income > 0 but missing in-range% still fails.
gates.passCriterion: fee_income > 0 for all runs with LP enabled. LP in-range percentage is present in run output and > 0%. Both conditions required; if LP is disabled in config, this gate is skipped.
gates.evidence: Strategy ran a full year with LP enabled and reported $0 in fees. Root cause: a bootstrap bypass had deployed the LP position out of range from day 1. The LP existed on-chain but the price never crossed into the range, so zero fees accrued. The in-range percentage check would have caught this on the first post-run validation — in-range% was 0% for the entire 8,760-hour run, making the root cause immediately visible without manual inspection of the position bounds.

gates.title: State Direct Mutation Prevention — All Transitions Through engine.trigger()
gates.description: Detect and block direct assignments to the state field outside of the designated transition method. All state machine transitions must go through engine.trigger() or the equivalent _set_state() method so that transition guards, entry/exit hooks, and audit logging are always executed. Applies to any project using a state machine pattern, not only DeFi simulations.
gates.check: SM-02: Pre-commit hook scans all Python/TypeScript files for direct assignment to the state field outside of the transition trigger method. Pattern to detect: `self\.state\s*=` (Python) or `this\.state\s*=` (TypeScript/JavaScript) appearing in any function other than trigger(), _set_state(), or __init__() / constructor(). The state field name is configurable in forgecraft.yaml (default: "state"). The list of permitted setter methods is configurable (default: ["trigger", "_set_state"]). All matches outside permitted methods must be reported with file, line number, and the surrounding function name. Zero tolerance — any match fails the hook. Also flag direct state assignments in test fixture setup: test files using `self.engine.state =` or `engine.state =` directly are masking the same bug class and must use a test-safe setter or a factory that produces an engine in the desired state.
gates.passCriterion: Zero occurrences of direct state field assignment outside permitted setter methods in source files. Zero direct state assignments in test fixture setup code.
gates.evidence: Direct state assignment in test fixture setup bypassed all transition guards. The engine entered RUNNING state without any position deployed because the test set self.state = "RUNNING" directly, skipping the guard that verifies LP + debt + short are all present. All guard conditions in the TRANSITIONS config were skipped. The bug passed all unit tests because the tests used the same anti-pattern in setup — direct state assignment in fixtures masked the broken guard by never invoking it. The fix required both removing the direct assignment from source and replacing direct assignment in all test fixtures with proper factory methods.

gates.title: Volatility Unit Convention Hook — Silent Gain/Loss Bug Prevention
gates.description: Detect code that double-scales volatility values by applying a conversion to a field that is already stored in the target unit. Prevents the single most common source of false crash triggers and missed recovery conditions in DeFi strategy simulations.
gates.check: Detect code that applies / sqrt(N) or * 100 to a field already stored as percentage-per-period. Flag any code path where a vol field annotated as pct_per_period is divided by sqrt() or multiplied by 100 without a unit conversion comment. Label the hook with the exact field names used in the codebase. Configurable: provide the list of annotated vol field names in .forgecraft/vol-fields.yaml.
gates.passCriterion: Zero double-scaling patterns detected on annotated vol fields. All sqrt() divisions on vol fields have a // unit: raw_decimal -> pct_per_period comment.
gates.evidence: Unit confusion in vol calculations is the single most common source of both false crash triggers and missed recovery conditions in DeFi strategies. A strategy that divides an already-annualized vol by sqrt(365) is 19x off on crash detection thresholds. The bug is invisible in unit tests because the wrong value passes all plausibility checks — the check must be semantic, not numeric.

gates.title: Walk-Forward Validation — Parameter Generalization Across Time Windows
gates.description: Before releasing optimized strategy parameters, verify that they generalize across at least three non-overlapping time windows. Catches parameters that are overfit to a specific market regime and will fail catastrophically when that regime ends.
gates.check: SIM-10: Before accepting any optimized parameter set as a release candidate, run the strategy with those parameters across at least 3 non-overlapping historical time windows of equal length. Windows must collectively cover distinct market regimes (trending, sideways, volatile). For each window, record: total_pnl, max_drawdown, sharpe_ratio, and liquidation_count. A parameter set passes only if: - It produces positive total_pnl in at least 2 of 3 windows. - It produces zero liquidations in all 3 windows. - The worst-window Sharpe is >= -0.5 (strategy does not catastrophically fail). Report per-window results in the release artifact. Optimizer journals must record the walk-forward result alongside the optimization score.
gates.passCriterion: Positive total_pnl in >= 2 of 3 non-overlapping time windows. Zero liquidations across all 3 windows. Worst-window Sharpe >= -0.5. Per-window results recorded in release artifact.
gates.evidence: Optimizer tuned parameters to a 1-year bull market period. The parameter set achieved exceptional Sharpe and drawdown metrics on the training window. When deployed into a sideways market, the strategy catastrophically failed — parameters sized positions assuming sustained directional movement that never materialized. Walk-forward validation across 3 windows (bull, sideways, bear) would have shown a 2/3 failure rate and zero liquidations only in the bull window, blocking release before deployment.

gates.title: Dependency CVE Audit — Pre-Commit Blocking Gate
gates.description: Audit all project dependencies for known CVEs before every commit. Block on HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerabilities in direct dependencies.
gates.check: Run the project's ecosystem audit tool before every commit. Block on HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs in direct dependencies. See tools-registry.yaml for ecosystem-specific commands: - Node.js: npm audit --audit-level=high - Python: pip-audit --severity high - Rust: cargo audit --deny warnings - Go: govulncheck ./... - Java: dependency-check --failBuildOnCVSS 7
gates.passCriterion: Zero HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs in direct dependencies. All transitive CVEs triaged with documented disposition (accepted, remediated, or suppressed with reason).
gates.evidence: The bcrypt -> @mapbox/node-pre-gyp -> tar CVE chain is a well-known example where a conceptually dev-only dependency introduced a production CVE. Pre-commit audit would have caught this at install time. Supply chain attacks via transitive dependencies are the fastest-growing vulnerability class as of 2025.

gates.title: Mutation Testing Gate — Pre-Release Test Quality Verification
gates.description: Verify that the test suite actually catches real bugs by injecting synthetic faults into the codebase and confirming that tests fail when the code is broken.
gates.check: Run mutation testing on changed files. Inject synthetic bugs: operator replacement (== to !=, + to -, < to <=), boundary inversion (off-by-one), and return value mutation (return null, return empty). If tests don't catch the injected bug, the test suite is checking the wrong things. Minimum score 80% on changed files. Tools: Stryker (JS/TS), PITest (Java), mutmut (Python), cargo-mutants (Rust).
gates.passCriterion: MSI >= 80% on changed files. No surviving mutants in critical path code (auth, permissions, financial calculations).
gates.evidence: GS experiments found 40-50% MSI on naive agent-generated test suites. Between 1-2 out of 3 agent-generated tests provide no real coverage. Explicit mutation gate consistently raises this to 80%+.

gates.title: OWASP ASVS Level 1 — Static Security Analysis at Commit Time
gates.description: Run static security checks on every commit to catch the most common vulnerability classes before they reach code review.
gates.check: Run a static security analyzer appropriate for the project's language. Checks must cover: (1) hardcoded secrets and credentials, (2) SQL/command injection patterns via string concatenation, (3) eval() with dynamic input, (4) dependency CVEs. See tools-registry.yaml for language-specific tools. These are LEVEL 1 checks: they run at every commit with no human review required.
gates.passCriterion: Zero HIGH/CRITICAL findings from static analyzer. All pre-commit hooks exit 0.
gates.evidence: Static analysis at commit time catches ~70% of OWASP Top 10 issues before they reach code review. SQL injection and hardcoded credential patterns are reliably detected statically without running the application.

gates.title: Playwright UI Smoke Test — Live Page Verification with Screenshot
gates.description: After every deploy or significant UI change, navigate to the live URL with Playwright, wait for all async data to load, assert that critical elements are present and non-empty, and save a full-page screenshot as a proof artifact. This catches an entire class of bugs that unit and API tests cannot see: blank pages, broken async fetches, CSS layout collapses, missing data sections, and rendering errors that only appear in a real browser against real infrastructure.
gates.check:
Write a Playwright script (scripts/smoke-test.js or tests/smoke/ui.spec.ts) that: 1. Navigates to the live URL with { waitUntil: "networkidle" } 2. Waits for async data to resolve (waitForFunction on a data-populated element) 3. Asserts critical elements: page title/heading, primary data section populated,
   no error messages visible (target specific error elements, not body.textContent
   which includes script source), navigation present
4. Takes a full-page screenshot and saves it as an artifact 5. Exits with code 1 on any assertion failure Run against staging before promotion, and against production within 5 minutes of deploy. Add to CI as a post-deploy step. Tool: Playwright (playwright.dev) — language-agnostic, works with any web UI.
gates.passCriterion: All element assertions pass. Screenshot artifact saved and visually confirms the page renders correctly. Script exits 0. Total run time < 60 seconds. No blank sections, no error messages, no spinner/loading states still active after the wait-for-data step.
gates.evidence: First run on genspec-portal caught two bugs invisible to unit tests: (1) Gates with multiple tags rendered once per tag — the page was visually cluttered with duplicates. Zero unit tests would catch this; it requires seeing the rendered output. (2) A naive body.textContent error check matched the JS source code string 'Failed to load taxonomy.json' — triggering a false positive that masked the real check. Both bugs found and fixed before any user saw the page. Pattern holds: the gap between "tests pass" and "page works" is exactly what Playwright closes.
